text: Welcome to this video which documents a robotic left-sided excision of a bronchogenic cyst with sutured repair. This was done at James Cook University Hospital by Mr. Joel Dunning. We have no conflicts of interest to declare. This patient is a thirty-five-year-old lady that had reported experiencing an intermittent and reedy productive cough for several years. It was very offensive and troublesome in nature. She also reported intermittent chest infections over the years. This is her CT scan and we've stopped at her bronchogenic cyst, which is just where the trachea bifurcates into the left and right main bronchi. And this is her preoperative bronchoscopy, where just a couple of centimeters distal to the carina on the left, there's a tiny hole just at about two o'clock.

The surgical team uses the Cifolia V port technique for port placement, with a 5mm thoracic grasper positioned anteriorly and the camera placed more posteriorly. The surgeon holds a Maryland instrument with bipolar energy in the right hand and Catiae forceps with a soft tip in the left hand.

The surgeon uses a swab in the chest for safety and cleaning small amounts of blood. Dissection of the inferior pulmonary ligament progresses past the pulmonary vein, revealing the left pulmonary artery. The vagus nerve is gently grasped and preserved, with its pulmonary branches divided and retracted posteriorly. Dissection continues down the left main bronchus, exposing the collapsed cyst sac due to its airway connection under negative pressure. The Maryland is initially used for dissection, followed by scissors to achieve fresh edges on the bronchus, minimizing damage.

The surgeon opens the cyst, revealing its depth and a clean cystic cavity. A small hole is visible in the membranous portion of the left main bronchus, resembling the preoperative bronchoscopic view. Robotic scissors are used for the final excision, and mediastinal tissue is mobilized to cover the hole with a single 4-0 Vicryl suture.

Two stitches are placed into the membranous part of the bronchus and stitched over the defect. A small amount of Evisil glue is applied over the wound. A drain is inserted, and the lungs are gently inflated with twenty centimeters of water pressure, confirming full inflation and completing the cyst excision.
The patient should recover from this procedure very rapidly with minimal post-operative pain. Thank you very much for watching and please feel free to reach out to us if you have any questions.
